fields.title: The Apartment
fields.body: Home. Relative term. The radiator clanks. The window looks out on a brick wall. There's a corkboard on the back of the door where you've started pinning job leads. Old habits die. New ones move in.

fields.title: Corky's
fields.body:
Corky's has been here longer than most of the buildings around it. The sign outside is missing a letter that nobody remembers. Inside it's dark the way a bar should be — not romantic, just honest about what the light would show. Two old men have been nursing pints in the corner booth since before you were born. Sal is behind the bar, doing what Sal does: keeping the glasses clean and his head down.

There's a board on the wall near the back with notices pinned to it. Some of them have been there long enough to curl at the corners.

fields.title: The Westside
fields.body:
The Westside runs on a corner economy that's been here longer than the current crew running it. Dense blocks, deep roots, everybody knowing everybody's business and most people pretending they don't. The Kings operate here the way water operates in a pipe — you feel the pressure without seeing the source.

Things move out here. People too, if they're not careful about where they stand.

fields.title: The Strip
fields.body:
The Strip runs six blocks of bars, restaurants, and storefronts that have learned to look legitimate without trying too hard. High foot traffic. Visible money. The kind of street where a conversation in a doorway could be anything and usually is something. The Backstreet Boys work the front end. Whatever else happens here happens behind it.

Nobody owns the Strip outright, which means everybody's always auditioning.

fields.title: The Brief
fields.body:
Morris calls at seven-thirty on a Wednesday. There's a bar in the Flats called Corky's — Dutch's people have been running product through it for three years, high foot traffic, regulars who know how to not see things. The owner, a guy named Sal, inherited it from a friend who left town. Sal has recently decided he wants the bar clean. He told Dutch's dealers to stop coming in. One of them got mouthy and Sal threw him out.

Dutch is not angry. Morris would tell you if Dutch were angry. Dutch just wants it resolved — the bar stays open, the dealers go back, Sal understands how the Flats works. Morris says this the way he says everything: like he's reading from an index card and is mildly satisfied with the index card.

He hangs up before you can ask what happens if Sal keeps pushing.

fields.title: The Job
fields.body:
Morris calls at seven in the morning, which already tells you something — Morris is a noon-at-the-earliest kind of person, and a seven a.m. call means the job is time-sensitive or something went wrong upstream and you're about to become part of it.

Package pickup at a lockup on Crane Street. Deliver it to an address on the other side of the Flats, Aldwick Road, third floor. Hand it to whoever answers. He says don't open it and don't stop anywhere, in the same tone he'd use to tell you the weather. Three hundred when it's done.

He hangs up. You've never met Morris in person. You're not sure that would clarify anything.

fields.title: Four Days Out
fields.body:
Four days out and the city has already made its position clear: it didn't miss you. The apartment is exactly how you left it, which means the water stain on the ceiling is still shaped like a boot, the radiator still clanks like it's trying to tell you something, and the stack of mail by the door is three years deep and entirely bills. The envelope of cash you came home with is already gone. Your parole officer wants you in legitimate work by the end of the month. Your old crew hasn't called.

All things considered, it could be worse. You're working on figuring out how.

Then your phone buzzes on the counter. Your sister's name on the screen — Vickie — and three years of silence sitting behind it like a held breath. She's calling at nine on a Tuesday night, which means either something has gone wrong or something is about to, and with Vickie the line between those two has always been thin. You stare at it for two rings.

fields.title: The Call
fields.body:
Morris calls at noon on the dot. No introduction — you know who it is. He says there's a package on Crane Street, a laundromat called Presto's, ask for Pete. Deliver it to the lockup on Decker, unit twelve, two hundred on completion. He stays on the line long enough to confirm you've heard him, then hangs up.

You don't know what's in the package. That's the job.

fields.title: Morris Calls
fields.body:
Morris calls mid-afternoon.

He gives you an address in the Westside, another on the Strip, and a final drop that he describes as “self-explanatory,” which is not a phrase Morris uses unless he has decided you don’t need the explanation.

You ask what you’re carrying.

“Package,” Morris says.

A pause. Then:

“Complete the sequence. Do not improvise.”

He hangs up.

The package is already outside your door.

fields.title: The Ask
fields.body: The contact finds you at the corner, not the other way around. Kings-adjacent, mid-level, the kind of person who passes messages without being the message. He says it quietly and without preamble: someone's been running product two blocks into Kings territory from the Flats side. Small enough that the council hasn't moved on it officially. Big enough that the corner crews are counting and comparing. He says the council wants it stopped before it becomes something they have to respond to formally, because a formal response means a formal escalation and nobody wants that. He says they want someone outside the Kings to look at it. He says they're asking you.

fields.title: Sal Mentions It
fields.body: Sal's wiping down the bar when he says it. Not to you specifically — just out loud, the way he says things. Danny Kowalski hasn't been in for two weeks. Tuesday, Thursday, every week for eleven years, and then nothing. Sal sets the cloth down and picks up a glass and doesn't say anything else. He's not asking you to do anything. He's just saying it. You finish your drink and think about it for longer than you probably should.

fields.title: The Corner
fields.body: There's an electronics shop two blocks down from where you're standing. You've walked past it three times this week without meaning to. Small place, independent, the kind that sells phone cases and cheap cables and doesn't ask questions. The till is visible from the street. So is the back room door, which is never fully closed. Tonight the Strip is doing what it does — high foot traffic, nobody watching anything. You could move now. Or you could watch it a little longer.

fields.title: Sal Tells You
fields.body: Sal puts the flash drive on the bar between you like he's setting down a coaster. Small thing. Cheap plastic, the kind you get in a pack of ten. He says a guy came in last Tuesday, ran up forty dollars, said he'd be back to cover it. Didn't come back. Left this behind on the table. Sal found it when he was clearing up. He held onto it because that seemed like the right thing to do and then two days later someone came in asking about the guy — plainclothes, didn't identify himself, but Sal's seen enough plainclothes to know plainclothes. And then yesterday someone else came in. Older, suit, asked the same questions differently. Sal says he told both of them nothing because he didn't know anything. He picks up his cloth. He says the guy's name was Fenn.

fields.title: The Call
fields.body: Burner number. No name. The voice is calm in the way that people are calm when they've already decided what they want and are just waiting for you to agree. There's a woman named Carla who works out of the noodle place on the corner. Someone's been watching her for three days — same car, same spot, same camera. The caller wants to know who hired the watcher. Then they want the watcher gone. They use that word specifically. Gone. You ask what it pays. They tell you. You don't ask anything else.

fields.title: The Approach
fields.body: A Kings soldier named Deon finds you at the corner. Not aggressive — careful. He says a guy from his crew named Calvin has been gone for four days. Not unusual for Calvin to go quiet but four days is different and the crew is starting to ask questions Deon doesn't know how to answer. He says Calvin was on Crane Street last Tuesday night — the block with the lockup — and came back different. Wouldn't say what he saw. Went quiet the next day and hasn't surfaced since. Deon says the crew thinks he's being leaned on by someone. He says he needs someone outside the Kings to find Calvin before the crew decides it's a loyalty question. He looks at you when he says outside the Kings. You understand what he means.

fields.title: The Bar
fields.body:
Corky's has been exactly this dark, exactly this quiet, for longer than anyone remembers. Old wood, a jukebox that still takes quarters, a TV above the register showing a game nobody's watching. Two men at the far end have been nursing pints since before you walked in and will be nursing them long after you leave.

Sal is behind the bar. Fifties, heavyset, the kind of tired that comes from running a place alone for four years. He clocks you the moment you come through the door — not with fear, just recognition. He's been expecting someone. Maybe not you specifically. Someone.

He doesn't stop wiping down the bar.

fields.title: Crane Street
fields.body:
The lockup is a steel door set into a brick wall between a tyre place and something that used to be a print shop. A man you've never seen opens it before you knock. Hands you a package wrapped in brown paper, taped at both ends — about the size of a shoebox and heavier than that. Doesn't speak. Closes the door.

You stand on Crane Street with it under your arm. Aldwick Road is twenty minutes on foot, straight through the Flats. Quiet enough at this hour.

fields.title: Her Voice
fields.body:
She doesn't say hello. She launches into it like she's been rehearsing, which she probably has — Vickie always rehearses the part where she asks for something and never quite the part that comes before it.

"There's this guy, Terrell. Young, maybe twenty-five, gold chain, acts like he's somebody. He keeps coming around saying I owe him for something Destiny ran up — my neighbor, it's complicated, I'll explain later. Second time he came by he put his hand on my door frame and told me if I didn't have something for him by the end of the week he was gonna break my legs." A pause. "He said it like that. Just said it. Like it was nothing."

You ask if she's okay. Long pause.

"I'm fine," she says. "I just need it to stop."

Another pause. Longer.

"I didn't want to call you," she says. "I want you to know that." Which is a very Vickie thing to say to someone she is currently calling.

fields.title: Crane Street
fields.body:
Pete is behind the counter sorting whites from darks like a man who has been trying very hard not to think about something for three days. He's mid-forties, soft around the middle, got a wedding ring and a look in his eyes that says he made one bad decision a long time ago and has been paying for it in small installments ever since.

He sees you, checks the door behind you, and goes to the back without a word. Comes out with a sealed package the size of a shoebox wrapped in brown paper and hands it over the counter like it's something he found and doesn't want to keep finding.

He says: don't come back.

You weren't planning to.

fields.title: Westside
fields.body:
The Westside notices you before you finish noticing it.

Balconies, windows, people on stoops who don’t look at you directly but also don’t look away. The kind of attention that doesn’t ask questions because it already has answers.

A guy across the street leans against a railing, watching you without watching you.

“You’re not from here,” he says.

fields.title: The Block
fields.body: Two blocks west of the Kings' established edge. A corner that's been neutral for years, the kind of neutral that holds because both sides agree it does. Someone's been working it for three weeks — not loudly, not flagrantly, just consistently. You watch it for an hour. The operation is small. One runner, careful, knows the timing. You clock the pickup point and the runner's route and you start working backward.

fields.title: What Sal Knows
fields.body: Not much. Danny's a retired machinist. Lives six blocks away on Crane Street. Drinks a pint of bitter and nurses a second one for two hours and tips exactly fifteen percent every time, which Sal finds both endearing and faintly insulting. No family that Sal knows about. Couple of friends who used to come in with him but stopped a few years back. Sal doesn't know why. He writes Danny's address on a bar napkin without being asked and slides it across the bar. Then he picks up his cloth and goes back to the glasses.

fields.title: Casing It
fields.body: Forty minutes. One staff member, young, changes his earbud every twenty minutes like clockwork. A delivery comes at nine and he goes to the back for it — two minutes, maybe three. The side alley has a fire exit that doesn't look alarmed. You file all of it away.

fields.title: The Drive
fields.body: You don't have anything to open it on at the bar so you sit with it for a minute and think about what Sal said. Two people asking about the same man within two days of each other, neither of them identifying themselves, both of them asking carefully. That's not a coincidence and it's not a small thing. Fenn found something he wasn't supposed to find and copied it onto a cheap flash drive and then left it on a table in Corky's, which means either he panicked or he was interrupted or both. You need to find him before the other two do.

fields.title: Find Carla
fields.body: The noodle place does a lunch special that runs out by noon. It's quarter past and Carla's at a table in the back with a ledger and a cup of tea she hasn't touched. Mid-forties, reading glasses on a chain, the particular stillness of someone who has spent a long time not reacting to things. She clocks you the second you walk in. You tell her someone's watching her. She says she knows. She's been watching him watch her for two days and didn't know what to do about it. She slides a napkin across the table with a license plate written on it. She says she wants to know who hired him too.

fields.title: What Deon Knows
fields.body: Not much more than he said. Calvin is twenty-two, been with the crew three years, reliable until last Tuesday. He has a girlfriend on the north Westside — Deon gives you her block — and a cousin who runs a card game on Fridays out of a basement on Mercer Street. Deon says Calvin didn't go home Tuesday night and hasn't called the girlfriend since. He says that part is new — Calvin calls her every day, has done for two years. Deon says find him before Friday because Friday is when people start drawing conclusions.

fields.title: Read The Room
fields.body:
You sit with it for twenty minutes. The bar tells you things.

Behind the register there's a photo — Sal with an older man, both of them grinning, the bar looking exactly the same in the background as it does now. Dennis, probably. On the shelf below it: a stack of envelopes. Cellophane windows, return addresses from places that expect money. More than a few. The kind you leave on the shelf when you're not ready to open them.

One of the regulars at the end calls Sal by name when he needs a refill. Sal knows his drink without being told. Knows the other one's too. The two of them have been doing this long enough that they don't need to talk about it.

The envelopes are the only thing in the room that doesn't fit.

fields.title: Aldwick Approach
fields.body:
Aldwick Road is residential, four-storey blocks either side, cars parked tight to the kerb. You're half a block from the address when you clock him — young guy, maybe twenty, leaning against a car on the wrong side of casual. Eyes on you since you turned the corner.

There's another one in the doorway of the building you're heading to. He's not a resident. The way he's standing says he's been there a while.

They know what you're carrying. They knew before you got here.

fields.title: Why You
fields.body:
She lets the silence run for a moment, like she's deciding how honest to be.

"Because you're the only person I know who can actually do something about it," she says. Then, quieter — "And because you owe me. You owe me about a thousand."

It lands exactly like she means it to. You don't argue. There's nothing useful to argue with.

fields.title: The Fork
fields.body:
Decker Avenue runs straight through the middle of the Flats. Fifteen-minute walk, broad daylight, direct. You've done it a hundred times.

You're halfway there when you spot the patrol car sitting at the intersection of Decker and Fifth — Blues, two officers, nobody moving. They're not doing anything. That's the thing about the Blues when they're sitting still: they're watching.

You can go around. Through the back streets off Marsh Lane — longer, slower, but out of sight. Or you can keep walking. You've got nothing on you they can legally touch. Probably.

fields.title: Hand Off
fields.body:
The door opens before you knock.

A man takes the package, disappears, and comes back ten seconds later with it like nothing changed.

“Next,” he says.

It weighs the same.

That’s the problem.

fields.title: The Trail
fields.body: The runner leads you three blocks before he makes a pickup from a lockup on Crane Street. You stay back and watch the handoff. The person making the delivery is young, Flats-side, wearing a jacket you recognize — not the person, the jacket. Dutch's people have a particular source for their outerwear and it's specific enough to be a tell if you know what you're looking at. You're not certain. You need to be certain before you do anything with this.

fields.title: Crane Street
fields.body: Six-story walkup. Danny's on the fourth floor. You knock. Long pause. Then the chain goes on before the door opens — just a crack, just enough. Danny is mid-sixties, smaller than you expected, wearing a cardigan that's seen better decades. He looks at you the way people look at strangers at their door when they're already scared of something else. You tell him Sal sent you. The chain stays on. He says he's fine. He says tell Sal he's fine. Behind him you can see a window with the curtain drawn in the middle of the afternoon.

fields.title: The Approach
fields.body: The store is exactly what it looked like from the street. One guy behind the counter, one earbud in, not particularly interested in anything. The back room door is ajar. You can see the edge of a desk and a lockbox sitting on it. Three ways to do this.

fields.title: Find Fenn
fields.body: Fenn isn't crew, isn't connected, isn't the kind of person who has a usual place to go to ground. What he has is a cousin who runs a laundromat off Decker Avenue and a habit of staying somewhere familiar when things go wrong. You work the thread carefully — not too many questions, not too fast, nothing that gets back to the wrong person before you're ready.

fields.title: The Watcher
fields.body: The car is a grey sedan parked in the dry cleaner's loading bay — closed for renovation, same as it's been for fourteen months. The watcher is inside it. Late forties, jacket that was expensive once, a camera with a long lens on the passenger seat. He sees you coming and doesn't move, which means he's either very calm or he's already made a call. You tap on the window. He rolls it down an inch. His eyes go to your hands first, then your face.

fields.title: The Girlfriend
fields.body: Her name is Aisha. She opens the door with her phone in her hand like she's been waiting for a call. When she sees you're not Calvin she puts the phone in her pocket and waits. You tell her Deon sent you. She says she doesn't know where he is. You believe her. She says he called her Monday — the night before Crane Street — and he sounded fine. Normal. Then nothing. She says the last thing he said was that he had to do something Tuesday night, a favor for someone on the crew, and he'd call her after. She says he didn't sound worried. She pauses. She says he sounded like someone who hadn't decided yet whether to be worried.

fields.title: Trail Success
fields.body: Cunning check passed. Harlan Street corner boy. Young, maybe nineteen, running a side operation that Dutch almost certainly doesn't know about — this is too sloppy, too visible, too much of exactly the kind of thing Dutch doesn't allow. He's freelancing. That changes the shape of this considerably.

fields.title: Found Fenn
fields.body: Cunning check passed. The laundromat. Back office, behind the industrial dryers, sitting on a folding chair with his jacket still on like he's been ready to leave for three days. Fenn is mid-thirties, unremarkable, the kind of face that doesn't stay in your memory. He looks at you and immediately starts explaining, which tells you he's been rehearsing this conversation and just needed someone to have it with.

fields.title: Trail Failure
fields.body: Cunning check failed. You lost the thread. What you have is a jacket and a lockup address and the knowledge that whoever's running this is Flats-connected. It's enough to act on but not enough to be clean about it.

fields.title: Too Late
fields.body: Cunning check failed. The cousin already talked — to the plainclothes officer, two hours ago. Fenn's location is no longer a secret you have and they don't. You still have the drive. That's something. But the clock is different now and the people moving on it are moving faster than you.

fields.title: Sal Face To Face
fields.body:
Sal puts the cloth down. He doesn't fold his arms — he just stops, and looks at you, and waits. He's done pretending he doesn't know why you're here.

He knows who sent you. Dutch's people have been coming in for three years and he should have said something sooner, he knows that too. He doesn't want trouble. He just wants to run his bar. He says that last part in a voice that's already accounted for how it sounds to someone like you.

Behind him: the photo, the envelopes, the jukebox cycling to something slow. Nobody changes it.

fields.title: The Ambush
fields.body:
The one by the car gets there first. He's quick and he's done this before but he leads with his right and you read it. The one from the doorway hangs back — waiting, watching, letting his guy do the work.

Behind him, a third. Older than the other two. Watching all of it with his arms folded. He doesn't move.

fields.title: Terrell
fields.body:
Vickie's building is a six-storey walkup off Decker Avenue, the kind of street that's been the same for forty years and is starting to notice. Terrell is exactly where she said he'd be — leaning against the stoop railing with the specific posture of someone who has decided that particular railing belongs to him now. Young. Maybe twenty-five. Gold chain, clean sneakers, the kind of eyes that clock you the second you turn the corner and don't look away after.

He watches you come without moving. When you're close enough he tilts his head.

"You lost?" he says.

fields.title: Back Streets
fields.body:
The back route adds ten minutes and takes you through a block of loading docks and shuttered units that see more foot traffic after midnight than before noon. Nobody's around. You keep the package close and your pace steady.

There's a moment — halfway through, crossing an open lot — where you feel exposed in a way you can't account for. Like something is watching that you can't see. You hold that feeling for about five seconds and then you keep moving, because that's what you do with feelings like that.

fields.title: The Strip
fields.body:
The Strip moves fast enough that nobody has time for you.

A storefront that looks expensive until you look closer.

“You’re early,” a man says.

You’re not.

fields.title: The Confrontation Setup
fields.body: You go back to the lockup the next evening. The corner boy is there — alone this time, counting something. He sees you and his hand moves toward his jacket and then stops when he reads your expression. You're not Kings. You're not Blues. He's trying to work out what you are and what that means for the next thirty seconds.

fields.title: The Debt
fields.body: He stands in the doorway and tells you in the flat voice of someone who has been rehearsing this: it's three hundred dollars, it belonged to a friend of his who died eight months ago, somehow it became his problem, two guys have come by twice now and the second time they broke something in the hallway. He says he's handling it. He is clearly not handling it. He says he doesn't want any trouble. You tell him you're not trouble. He looks at you for a long moment, like he's deciding whether that's ever been true of anyone who's said it.

fields.title: The Bluff
fields.body: You're a customer. Browsing. The guy behind the counter is young and not particularly switched on. The back room is twelve feet away. You just need him looking somewhere else long enough.

fields.title: Fenn Talks
fields.body: He found the records by accident — he does occasional bookkeeping work, legitimate, and a client handed him a folder that had something in it that shouldn't have been there. Names, dates, amounts. Someone skimming from operations he recognized immediately and wished he hadn't. He copied what he could onto the drive before he understood what he was looking at. Then he understood what he was looking at and he ran. He left the drive at Corky's because he panicked and because he goes there sometimes and it felt like somewhere safe, which he knows now was stupid. He says he just needs to get out of the city. He's not asking for anything else.

fields.title: The Squeeze
fields.body: You tell him you're not the police and you're not going to hurt him and both of those things are true as long as he tells you what you need to know. You keep your voice level. You put one hand on the roof of the car. The trick is making the space feel smaller without moving.

fields.title: The Favor Detail
fields.body: She doesn't know what the favor was. But she remembers he mentioned a name — not a crew name, just a first name, the corner boy who asked him to do it. The name she gives you is the same name attached to a jacket on Crane Street last Tuesday. The same freelancer. The same lockup. Calvin wasn't a witness to something. Calvin was doing the run for the corner boy — one night, cash in hand, favor returned. And something went wrong on Crane Street that night that Calvin came back from and hasn't talked about since.

fields.title: Bluff Success
fields.body: Nerve check passed. He holds for a second, squinting at the shelf like he's trying to remember something. Then he turns back around and you're already through the door.

fields.title: Squeeze Success
fields.body: Nerve check passed. He talks in the clipped way of someone who has decided that cooperation is the least bad option available to him. A law firm on the Hill. Client privilege means he doesn't know who specifically, just the billing address. He writes it on a card and hands it through the gap in the window. Then he asks what happens now.

fields.title: Bluff Failure
fields.body: Nerve check failed. He laughs — short, sharp, not friendly — and his hand comes up from under the counter with something in it. You're already moving but the door between you is closing fast.

fields.title: Squeeze Failure
fields.body: Nerve check failed. He gets the phone halfway to his ear before you make a decision. The window isn't going to stop you and he knows it. You go around to the passenger side.

fields.title: Lean On Sal
fields.body:
You lay out the geography. Dutch has been in the Flats longer than Sal has owned this bar. The dealers coming back isn't a negotiation — the only question is whether Sal makes it easy or makes it difficult, and the difference between those two things is significant and specific and he already knows what it is.

You keep your voice down. The trick is making sure he has to lean in slightly to hear you.

He's deciding whether the bar is worth it.

fields.title: Darius
fields.body:
He's still standing there. Arms still folded. He looks at his guy on the ground, then back at you, with the expression of a man who has just revised an estimate downward and is being professional about it.

He says his name is Darius. Says he's not here on Kings business — which is a specific thing to volunteer. Says the package was a personal opportunity and he misjudged the job. He's not apologising. He's accounting.

He looks at the package under your arm. Then at you.

Tell Dutch he says hello. Then he walks. No hurry. Doesn't check if you're watching.

fields.title: Squeeze
fields.body:
You tell him you're there for the woman on the third floor. That whatever she owes, she doesn't owe it anymore. You don't raise your voice. You don't need to — the point isn't volume, it's clarity. You look at him the way you look at something you've already decided about and let him work out the rest himself.

Terrell is listening. Behind the performance of not being impressed, something is doing the math.

fields.title: Roll Success
fields.body: Cunning check passed. You come out on Decker two blocks south of the patrol. They're still sitting at Fifth, not looking your way. Unit twelve is another three minutes on foot.

fields.title: Strip Hand Off
fields.body:
He circles the package like it might explain itself.

Ten seconds in the back. Voices.

He hands it back.

“You didn’t get this from us.”

fields.title: The Negotiation
fields.body: You lay it out for him. He's two blocks into Kings territory. The council knows. The only reason this hasn't already become something bad is because they wanted it handled quietly, and quiet is still available to him if he walks away from this corner tonight and doesn't come back. You tell him Dutch doesn't know he's here and suggest he think carefully about whether Dutch finding out would be better or worse than what's currently on the table.

fields.title: The Corner
fields.body: It doesn't take long. Two corner boys, working the block two streets over. Young — early twenties at most. They're not Kings, not BSB, just muscle hired to follow up on a debt that got sold down the chain until it landed with someone who needed the three hundred more than they needed the hassle. They clock you coming. The taller one crosses his arms. The shorter one doesn't move at all, which is worse.

fields.title: The Fight Setup
fields.body: No more pretending. The counter isn't going to help him as much as he thinks it will. You've done this before. The trick is being faster than the moment when he decides to do something about it.

fields.title: The Decision
fields.body: Three options. The drive goes to the plainclothes officer and becomes evidence and Fenn becomes a witness and the Buccos work out where it came from eventually and Corky's is in that story. Or the drive goes to the Bucco associate and Fenn doesn't get out of the city and you get paid and you know what you paid for. Or you tell Fenn to go, you break the drive, and neither of the two people asking gets what they came for. The last option doesn't pay anything. It does mean you can look Sal in the eye tomorrow.

fields.title: What To Do With Him
fields.body: He has the name you need. He has a phone that may or may not have already sent something. He's looking at you through the window with the expression of a man doing math.

fields.title: The Search
fields.body: You work the cousin's card game angle. The game runs Thursday nights too, not just Fridays, and the cousin isn't hard to find if you know where card games happen in this part of the Westside. You ask around carefully — not Calvin's name, just the shape of someone who needs a quiet place for a few days and has family who might provide it.

fields.title: The Decision
fields.body: You still have the drive. Fenn is somewhere the plainclothes officer is now looking and you can't change that. What you can change is what happens to the evidence. Hand it to the Blues and it becomes a case and Corky's is in that story. Hand it to the Bucco associate and whoever they find first pays for all of it. Break it and nobody gets what they came for, which is the cleanest outcome available from a bad position.

fields.title: Leverage
fields.body:
You ask him about the envelopes. He goes still.

You tell him you're not here to make it worse. Dutch's operation running through the bar is quiet income on top of what the bar makes — not nothing, when the cellophane windows are stacking up. You're not telling him anything he doesn't already know. You're just saying it out loud so you're both looking at the same picture.

He doesn't like it. Liking it isn't the ask.

fields.title: Squeeze Success
fields.body: Nerve check passed. He holds it for a second. Then something in his posture settles, the way a bluff does when the other person calls it and there's nothing left to protect. He doesn't say anything. He doesn't have to.

fields.title: Negotiation Success
fields.body: Nerve check passed. He walks. Leaves the lockup, leaves the corner, doesn't look back. You wait twenty minutes to be sure and then you go find the contact and tell him it's done. You don't tell him it was Harlan Street. You don't tell him it was a freelancer. You just say it's done and let him decide what that's worth.

fields.title: The Extraction Setup
fields.body: He comes out of the car with his hands up, which is either smart or practiced — you're not sure which. The camera stays on the seat. He's bigger than he looked through the glass and he's already decided something. You can see it in the way he's standing.

fields.title: Search Success
fields.body: Cunning check passed. The basement on Mercer. The card game isn't running tonight — it's just Calvin, sitting in the dark with the lights off, which you know because you can see the glow of his phone under the door. You knock. Long pause. Then the door opens.

fields.title: Negotiation Failure
fields.body: Nerve check failed. He goes for the jacket and you're already moving. The lockup is tight and he's younger than you but fear and bad judgment are their own kind of fuel.

fields.title: Search Failure
fields.body: Cunning check failed. The cousin stonewalls you — politely, firmly, in the way of someone who has been asked to stonewall and is doing it carefully. You don't get a location. But you get enough to know Calvin is close and scared and the cousin is the only person he's trusted with where. You go back to Deon.

fields.title: Squeeze Failure
fields.body: Nerve check failed. He laughs — short, sharp, meant to be seen. "I'm gonna crush you, you little twerp," he says. He means it.

fields.title: Deon's Move
fields.body: Deon listens. Then he makes a call. Fifteen minutes later the cousin calls him back and gives up the address, because Deon asking directly is a different weight than a stranger asking. Deon gives you the address and says go alone — Calvin doesn't need to see crew right now. You go to the basement on Mercer.

fields.title: Sal Talks
fields.body:
He talks. Once he starts he doesn't stop for a while.

He inherited Corky's four years ago from Dennis, who was his closest friend for twenty years and who left town in a hurry for reasons Sal has never pressed on. The bar wasn't making money then. It's barely making money now. He's kept it going because it was Dennis's and because the regulars have nowhere else to go and because some part of him still thinks it might turn around.

The dealers bother him less than the principle. It's his bar. If he can't decide who comes in then it's just a bar he works at.

He stops. Looks at the cloth in his hands like he forgot he was holding it. Says he doesn't know why he's telling you this.

fields.title: The Delivery
fields.body:
A woman answers. Forties, precise, looks at the package and not at you. Takes it. Closes the door.

That's it. That's the job.

You stand in the stairwell for a moment. Your knuckles are split. There's a noise coming from behind the door that might be a scale. You don't know what was in the package. You still don't ask.

You take out your phone to call Morris. You can tell him exactly what happened — Darius, the Kings, the name dropped at the end. Dutch would want to know. Or you can keep it simple. Package delivered. Job done.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body:
Terrell squares up before you've finished closing the distance. He's done this before — you can see it in how he sets his feet, how his hands come up. He's got reach and he knows it. What he doesn't have is patience.

Somewhere above you a window slides shut. The city, taking no position.

fields.title: Roll Fail
fields.body:
Cunning check failed. You misread the back streets and come out directly across from the patrol. One of the officers clocks you before you can adjust — the package tucked under your arm, you moving a little too deliberately for a man with nowhere to be.

He doesn't stop you. He just watches. Writes something down.

fields.title: Something Off
fields.body:
The timestamps don’t line up.

That means either the job is wrong or the job is working exactly as intended.

You’re already too far in to find out which.

fields.title: The Fight Setup
fields.body: Small space. One way in, one way out. He's made his decision and so have you.

fields.title: The Negotiation
fields.body: You explain the situation. Danny didn't run up this debt, his dead friend did, and the friend is no longer available to collect from. You suggest that passing this particular problem back up the chain is probably worth more to everyone than three hundred dollars from a retired machinist in a cardigan. You keep it reasonable. You keep it calm. The taller one is listening. The shorter one is still not moving.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body: Fast and ugly. The Strip noise bleeds in under the door and covers most of it.

fields.title: Clean
fields.body: Fenn is gone. The drive is in pieces on the laundromat floor. You go back to Corky's and sit at the bar and Sal pours without asking. You tell him the guy won't be coming back but his tab is settled and nobody needs to know anything else. Sal nods. He picks up his cloth. The two old men in the corner are still nursing their pints. The jukebox plays something from one era. Everything is exactly as it was, which is the point.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body: The loading bay is tight. The car is between you for the first two seconds and then it isn't.

fields.title: Calvin
fields.body: Twenty-two. Sitting on an upturned crate in the dark. He looks at you the way people look at someone when they've run out of places to go. He doesn't ask how you found him. He just says he didn't know what was in the lockup. He thought it was product, standard run, cash job. But when he got there the corner boy wasn't moving product. He was meeting someone — older, suit, unhurried. Calvin heard enough of the conversation to understand that the corner boy was passing information about Kings operations to someone who wasn't Kings. He panicked and left and hasn't known what to do with it since.

fields.title: Counter Offer
fields.body:
The offer is simple. Sal keeps Corky's clean on weeknights — his regulars, his rules. Weekend afternoons, Dutch's people can work the room. It's not what Dutch asked for but it's not nothing, and more importantly it's an arrangement Sal can live with. Operations that run on resentment leak. Dutch knows that, even if you've never met Dutch and are making an educated guess about what Dutch knows.

Sal writes it down on a bar napkin. You don't tell him that doesn't make it a contract. He needs the napkin to be official, so the napkin is official.

You take a photo of it on your phone. Tell him you'll pass it up the chain. Tell him Dutch is a reasonable man.

fields.title: Taken
fields.body:
You come back to yourself on the pavement. The package is gone. Darius is gone. His guys are gone. The street looks exactly like it did before — cars at the kerb, windows closed, nobody saw anything.

You call Morris. He listens to the whole thing without interrupting. When you finish he says he'll call you back. He doesn't call back.

fields.title: Negotiation Success
fields.body: Nerve check passed. The taller one says the debt's not worth the conversation and that's the end of it. Not generous — practical. They walk. You watch them go for long enough to be sure, then you take the long way back to Crane Street.

fields.title: The Side Door
fields.body: The fire exit opens easier than it should — the latch has been taped back, probably by the staff member who smokes back here. You're in the stockroom before anyone knows the building has changed. The back room is one door ahead. You can hear the guy at the counter running his mouth to someone on the phone.

fields.title: Evidence
fields.body: The plainclothes officer takes the drive without thanking you. He asks how you came to have it. You tell him you found it. He looks at you for a moment with the particular expression of someone filing something away for later and then he leaves. Three weeks later you hear that Corky's came up in a deposition — not as a subject, just as a location. Sal reads about it in a filing someone shows him. He doesn't say anything to you. He doesn't need to.

fields.title: Fight Victory
fields.body: He's on the ground by the rear wheel. You go through his jacket. A wallet, a phone with three missed calls on the screen, and a folded invoice with a billing address — a law firm on the Hill. Someone with money and a reason to watch Carla. You take the invoice and the phone. He's breathing. Someone called before you got here, which means someone knows this went wrong.

fields.title: The Suit
fields.body: Calvin didn't recognize him. But he described him well enough — older, methodical, the kind of large that comes with a suit — that you recognize the description. Bucco muscle. Which means the corner boy wasn't just freelancing on territory. He was selling Kings operational information to the Bucco Family. That's a different problem than a boundary violation. That's the kind of thing that doesn't stay quiet.

fields.title: Negotiation Failure
fields.body: Nerve check failed. The shorter one moves first and the taller one is half a second behind him. You don't have a lot of room to work with.

fields.title: Sal Folds Intimidated
fields.body:
He says fine. One word. Then he moves to the other end of the bar and starts restocking glasses and doesn't look at you again.

You sit there a moment longer than you need to. The two old men haven't moved. The jukebox is between songs.

fields.title: Done Straight
fields.body:
Morris calls back in an hour. Dutch wants the name again — Darius — and the description. You give both. Morris asks if he said anything else. You tell him about the not on Kings business. Another pause, longer than the first.

The three hundred comes through plus a fifty on top. Information tax, Morris calls it, in a tone that suggests he finds this categorisation accurate and sufficient.

You don't know what Dutch does with the name. You told the truth and got paid for it.

fields.title: Backed Off
fields.body:
Terrell thinks about it for a moment — genuinely thinks, which is more than you expected — and then he pockets his phone and goes. No exit line. No last look. The ones who are actually scared never need the exit line.

You text Vickie two words. It's done.

She replies in three minutes with a thumbs up. A single thumbs up, after three years. You stare at it longer than makes sense and then put your phone away.

fields.title: Main Road
fields.body:
You walk past the two Blues officers posted at the corner. They see you. One of them gets on the radio. You don't run — running ends this differently — so you keep moving, even, unhurried, eyes forward, and count the seconds until you're past the intersection.

The package gets there. So does whatever they radioed in.

fields.title: New Build
fields.body:
Clean storefronts. Old problems underneath.

A man steps into your path.

“That’s not yours,” he says.

No anger. Just certainty.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body: The lockup is four walls and a concrete floor and not much else. He's fast and cornered, which is its own problem.

fields.title: The Fight Setup
fields.body: The taller one says something. You stop listening after the first word. The block is quiet enough that this is going to be noticed and loud enough from the street that nobody's going to come out to watch. Two of them. You've had worse math.

fields.title: The Back Room
fields.body:
Small room. Shelving units, a safe that's open and mostly empty, and a lockbox on the desk that isn't. You crack it — cash, and underneath the cash a ledger. Handwritten, two years of names and numbers. You recognize two of them and wish you didn't.

Then you hear breathing. There's someone in the corner behind the shelving unit. Young, maybe seventeen, sitting on an upturned crate with his knees pulled up. He looks at you. You look at him. He was clearly not supposed to be here either.

fields.title: Handled
fields.body: The associate takes the drive and hands you an envelope without counting out what's in it, which means it's enough that counting would be insulting. He says the matter is handled. He says it the way people say things when they mean something specific by handled. You go back to Corky's. You don't ask about Fenn. You don't hear about Fenn. That's probably the answer.

fields.title: Clean Exit
fields.body: You call the burner number from three blocks away. Read out the billing address. The line is quiet for a moment and then the caller says good and hangs up. You stop at the noodle place on the way back and tell Carla. She writes something in her ledger and doesn't say thank you, which is its own kind of thank you. The zip ties are still in your jacket pocket. You keep them.

fields.title: The Decision
fields.body: Calvin goes back to the crew either way — that part is settled. The question is what goes back with him. You know what he saw. You know what it means. Deon doesn't know yet. The crew doesn't know. The Kings council doesn't know. You have four options for how much of this moves and in which direction.

fields.title: Sal Folds Leveraged
fields.body:
He looks at the shelf for a long time. Then back at you. Then he gives a small nod — not agreement exactly, more like a man accepting a diagnosis he already suspected.

He says the dealers can come back. He says it like a business decision, which is what you gave him — a frame that wasn't pure capitulation. You leave him to it.

fields.title: Sal Holds Firm
fields.body:
He's not moving. The decision is already made and whatever you say next is noise. Some people get to a point where the fear stops working and all that's left is the thing they're protecting.

You don't push it further. You tell him Dutch will be in touch. He says he knows. You leave.

fields.title: Done Covered
fields.body:
Three hundred by end of day, same as promised. Morris doesn't ask for details. You didn't offer any.

Somewhere in the Westside, Darius is telling someone about a courier who wouldn't go down. Maybe he's telling the Kings council. Maybe he's keeping it private, same as you. You won't know until it matters.

The split knuckles take three days to close.

fields.title: Roughed Up Win
fields.body:
Terrell's on the ground. His friends, who had opinions about this earlier, have revised them and left. You go through his pockets while he's still deciding whether to do something about that — a hundred and fifty in crumpled bills and a pair of brass knuckles with somebody else's initials scratched into the base, which tells a small story you don't have time for right now.

Your ribs are going to have thoughts about this tomorrow. You pocket both and go.

You text Vickie. It's done. The message sits on read.

fields.title: Fight Victory
fields.body: He's down. The lockup is quiet. You go through what he left behind — enough to confirm the Harlan Street connection, nothing that names Dutch directly. You take what's useful and leave the rest. Then you find the contact and tell him the corner is clear. You don't say how. He doesn't ask. But the way he doesn't ask tells you he already knows more than he let on, which means the Kings had their own read on this and wanted to see what you'd do with it.

fields.title: Loose End
fields.body: You call the burner number. Read out the billing address. The caller says good and hangs up. You tell Carla. She nods and writes something down. Three days later you hear that the grey sedan came back, different plates, same spot. The watcher has a client who's paying enough to keep going. That's not your problem anymore. It might become someone else's.

fields.title: Done Dealers In
fields.body:
Morris calls that evening. Two hundred for the job. The dealers go back into Corky's starting Thursday. He says Dutch is satisfied, which from Morris means he says satisfied and hangs up.

The money goes in the drawer. There's another slip on the corkboard already — Morris works fast.

You don't go back to the bar. You don't have a reason to. Somewhere on the other side of the Flats, Sal is restocking glasses. The two old men at the end of the bar are still there, or they've gone home, and either way they'll be back tomorrow and the dealers will be back Thursday and nobody will say anything about it because that's the thing about Corky's. People learned a long time ago what not to notice.

fields.title: Done Lost
fields.body:
Morris doesn't call back that day. Or the next.

On the third day, a single text. Dutch wants the debt covered in work, not cash. There'll be a job. It won't be a good one.

The package is in whatever hands Darius was moving it to, doing whatever it was going to do. The corkboard is empty. You wait for Morris to put something on it.

fields.title: Done Clean
fields.body:
Back in the apartment. Radiator having its opinions. The boot-shaped water stain unchanged, indifferent, exactly where you left it.

You didn't have to break anything tonight. That used to be the baseline and somewhere along the way it became an achievement, which is either personal growth or a bad sign and you're not ready to decide which.

Vickie sends another message an hour later. Just: thank you. No punctuation, which means she agonised over it. You know her. She probably typed it six different ways.

The corkboard on the back of the door has one job pinned to it. The city is paying attention. That's something.

fields.title: Clean Delivery
fields.body:
The lockup smells like motor oil and old cardboard. Unit twelve has a combination padlock with the code scratched into the doorframe in pencil, which is either careless or confident. You set the package inside, lock up, and text the number Morris gave you. Delivered.

He replies in forty seconds. Acceptable. Then: Dutch knows your name. That's worth something. Then nothing.

There's a switchblade on the shelf above where you set the package. No note. You take it — it would be strange not to.

Two hundred cash arrives in the usual way, which you have now figured out is the usual way.

fields.title: Push Through
fields.body:
He doesn’t stop you.

That’s the whole decision.

You walk past.

fields.title: Done
fields.body: Clean. The corner goes quiet. Neither side has to acknowledge what happened or why. The cold war holds. The contact pays you in the parking structure off Decker and shakes your hand once, which is more than you expected. You don't mention Harlan Street. You don't mention Dutch. Some information is worth more sitting still than moving.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body: Two against one in a tight block. The shorter one is faster than he looks. The taller one makes up for it with commitment.

fields.title: The Exit
fields.body:
You're moving back through the store when you see it — a car idling outside that wasn't there ten minutes ago. Two people in the front seat not looking at anything in particular, which in this neighborhood means they're looking at everything. Could be nothing. Could be someone who owns this store keeping tabs on it in a way that doesn't show up on any roster.

The kid in the back room is going to make a decision in about thirty seconds.

fields.title: Burned
fields.body: You break the drive and walk away and for two days it seems like that was the right call. Then a Blues unit comes into Corky's and spends forty minutes asking Sal questions about a regular named Fenn who used the bar as a drop point for stolen financial records. Sal tells them nothing because he knows nothing, which is the one thing that went right. He calls you afterward and doesn't say anything for a long moment. Then he says he needs a few days. You give him the few days. The bar is still there when they're up. So is Sal. Things are different in a way neither of you names.

fields.title: Messy Exit
fields.body: You call the burner number from a payphone two blocks over. Read out the billing address. The caller says good and then says they heard it got complicated. You don't ask how they heard. They say the payment stands but next time keep it cleaner. The line goes dead. You tell Carla in passing. She doesn't look surprised, which means she already knew, which means she has sources you don't know about. That's interesting.

fields.title: Clean Return
fields.body: Calvin goes back. He tells the crew he needed a few days, personal reasons, nothing to do with the crew. Deon accepts it because accepting it is easier than not accepting it. The corner boy is still out there. The Bucco connection is still live. Nobody knows except you. That information is either an asset or a weight and right now you can't tell which.

fields.title: Done Sal Holds
fields.body:
Morris calls back two hours later. Dutch looked at the counter-offer. The answer is no — the dealers go back in on Dutch's terms or the conversation becomes a different one. There's no payout on this one.

Morris says Dutch appreciated the initiative. He says it the way he says everything: level, informational, not quite warm. He says there'll be more work. He says it carefully.

You still have the photo of the napkin on your phone. Sal built a counter-offer on a bar napkin and it lasted two hours.

fields.title: Done Rough
fields.body:
Back in the apartment. Ribs announcing themselves every time you breathe wrong. The hundred and fifty from Terrell's pocket on the counter next to the brass knuckles with the stranger's initials, which you should probably get rid of and probably won't.

Vickie never texted back. That's fine. You didn't do it for the reply.

The corkboard on the back of the door has one job pinned to it. Not a bad Tuesday, all things considered. The bar for that is low, but it's a bar.

fields.title: Messy
fields.body: The contact pays you and then mentions, almost in passing, that they had a feeling it was Flats-connected. You don't confirm it. You don't deny it. He nods like you've said something useful anyway. The Kings now know the direction if not the name. That's a shorter distance to Harlan Street than anyone needs right now. The cold war is a little colder.

fields.title: Fight Victory
fields.body: They're done. You check yourself for damage — nothing serious, nothing that won't close on its own. One of them says, from the ground, that the debt stands. You tell him to pass that up the chain and see what they say. Then you go back to Crane Street.

fields.title: Deon Knows
fields.body: Deon goes very still when you tell him. He asks you to say it again. Then he thanks you in the careful way of someone who is already thinking about what comes next and doesn't want you to see it. Calvin goes back to the crew. What Deon does with the information is Deon's business now. The council will know by morning. What happens to the corner boy after that is not something Deon says out loud.

fields.title: Done Sal Caved
fields.body:
Dutch handled it himself. Morris doesn't elaborate on what that means and you don't ask. What you know is that two days later the dealers are back in Corky's and Sal is still behind the bar and nothing visible has changed.

Morris sends a hundred. Half rate. You showed up and made the ask. That's apparently worth something — fifty less than something, but something.

Sal's still got the bar. That's probably what he'd say if you asked him.

fields.title: Beaten
fields.body:
You come to on the pavement outside Vickie's building, which is not where you intended to end up tonight. Ribs aching, wallet lighter — someone took the liberty while you were unavailable to object. The stoop light is still flickering. It was flickering before. It'll be flickering tomorrow.

You don't go up. You can't do that conversation right now, can't stand in Vickie's doorway looking like this and tell her it's handled. You just walk. The Flats swallows you up the way it always has — wide streets, low buildings, sky overhead doing nothing in particular — and after a while you stop thinking about it.

Your phone has no new messages.

fields.title: Hot Delivery
fields.body:
Package delivered. Morris's reply takes four minutes this time. Delivered. Blues logged a person of interest on Decker carrying a package. You. Pause. Dutch is aware. Another pause. A hundred and fifty.

Fifty less than agreed. You don't argue. There's nobody to argue with — Morris has already hung up.

The lockup has a switchblade on the shelf. You look at it. You don't take it. That feels right.

fields.title: The Drop
fields.body:
Clean.

That’s the only word for it.

A man takes the package. Doesn’t check it.

Ten seconds later, it’s gone.

“You’re done.”

fields.title: Burned
fields.body: You come to in the lockup. He's gone, the product is gone, the lockup is empty except for you. By the time you find the contact the corner is already hot — the Kings crews are counting and the word freelancer is moving through the wrong channels toward the word Harlan. You didn't start this. You didn't stop it either.

fields.title: The Choice
fields.body: Danny opens the door without the chain this time. You tell him the two guys won't be coming back. He nods slowly, like he was hoping for something he knew was too much to ask. Then he asks what it cost you. You think about the three hundred dollars and the conversation and whatever comes next for whoever owns that debt. Two options present themselves.

fields.title: Done
fields.body:
Clean. The Strip swallows you up the way you knew it would. The cash is real, the ledger is in your jacket, and nobody got a good look at anything. The car outside turned out to be nothing, or if it wasn't nothing it wasn't fast enough.

You don't know yet what's in the ledger. That's tomorrow's question.

fields.title: Burned
fields.body: You come to in the loading bay. The car is gone. His phone is gone. Your pockets are lighter than they were. Somewhere in the city a private investigator is explaining to his client that someone came asking, and the client is deciding what to do about that, and you don't know who the client is or what they're capable of. The noodle place is two blocks away. You don't go. You don't have anything to tell Carla.

fields.title: Council
fields.body: You bypass Deon and go straight to the council through the contact who gave you the job. It takes two hours and a waiting room and Ray Okafor asking you specific practical questions for twenty minutes. Then Curt says something short that everyone finishes what they're doing to hear. Calvin goes back to the crew. The corner boy's situation is resolved at a level above your pay grade, quickly and without announcement. You get paid twice — once for finding Calvin, once for what you brought back with him.

fields.title: Complications
fields.body:
You walk out the front and the car clocks you. Not immediately — there's a delay, like they're confirming something, which means the kid made his call faster than you expected. You're half a block away before the doors open. You don't run. Running is worse. You take three turns and duck into the dry cleaner's doorway — closed for renovation, fourteen months now — and wait, and after four minutes nothing comes past, and you walk the rest of the way in the direction that isn't your actual direction.

You've got the cash. You've got the ledger. But someone got a look at you, and someone in that car is going to describe what they saw to whoever owns that store.

fields.title: Sitting On It
fields.body: Calvin goes back with nothing. You keep what you know. Three days later the corner boy is gone — not the way the council would have done it, quietly, but visibly, messily, in a way that costs the Kings two blocks of credibility on the Flats border. Someone else connected the same dots you did and moved faster. The information you were sitting on is now just history. Deon doesn't blame you. He doesn't know you had it. That's almost worse.

fields.title: Clocked
fields.body:
Package delivered. Morris's reply comes in three minutes. Delivered. Blues patrol noted someone on the back route near Marsh. Description matches. A pause. A hundred and seventy-five.

Twenty-five short. You did everything right except the one thing. Morris doesn't explain the math — he doesn't need to. Dutch's people are careful because Dutch is careful, and the back route is supposed to be clean, and now there's a description floating around that matches yours.

fields.title: Parked Car
fields.body:
The car has been there long enough to belong.

You pass it once.

Nothing.

The second time, you feel it.

Not attention. Recognition.

You keep walking.

fields.title: Done
fields.body: You pull the cash out. He looks at it for a long moment and then he looks at you and says he doesn't understand why a stranger would do that. You tell him Sal's been wiping down the same glasses for eleven years waiting for him to come back in on Tuesday. You're not sure if that's the real reason. It'll do. He takes the cash. He says he'll be in Thursday. You go back to Corky's and tell Sal. Sal picks up his cloth.

fields.title: Burned
fields.body:
You come to outside. Someone dragged you out, which means someone didn't want the police called, which tells you something about who owns the store that you would have preferred to know going in. No cash. No ledger. The side of your face is making an argument you don't have a response to.

Two blocks away you stop and check your pockets out of habit. Empty. The Strip keeps moving around you, indifferent, unhurried, completely unimpressed.

fields.title: Scared Off
fields.body: You tell him the two guys are gone but the debt is still out there somewhere, and whoever owns it will eventually pass it to someone new. He should lie low, vary his routine, not open the door to strangers. He nods. He says he understands. He's been doing that for two weeks already. You go back to Corky's. You don't tell Sal the details. He doesn't ask. He pours you one on the house, which he's never done before, and goes back to his glasses.

fields.title: Crossing Lines
fields.body:
Back home.

Cash. Zonk.

The route stays in your head.

Morris said acceptable.

That’s what you get.

fields.title: Beaten
fields.body: You wake up on the block. They're gone. Nothing broken that won't heal, nothing taken except the evening. You go back to Crane Street anyway. Danny answers on the first knock this time. He looks at you and then he looks past you and he says quietly that he appreciates it but he doesn't want anyone else coming around. He says it like he means it. He closes the door. You go back to Corky's and sit at the bar and don't say anything and Sal doesn't ask.
